Reproduced from PRD text: the lord is "Kurosawa Munenori" (§1.8 line 697; §5 line 4688) — apex rank-gatekeeper, non-martial, ledger-minded; the master-at-arms/drillmaster is "Jūbei" (line 703/4689), whose creed (4629) is the T0 emotional spine. Historically these are exactly Yagyū Munenori (shogunal sword instructor) and his son Yagyū Jūbei Mitsuyoshi (the legendary one-eyed swordsman) — two of the most iconic names in samurai sword culture, landing on the lord and the sword teacher respectively. The §6.6 real-name denylist (lines 5965-5969, 992-998) explicitly scopes to real SURNAMES/PLACES and intra-cast collisions only, so famous GIVEN names are unguarded; grep for "Yagyū/Yagyu" across prd.md, prd_human_feedback.md, and decisions.md returns ZERO hits — no acknowledgment anywhere. All factual sub-claims verify.

Why severity is low (adversarial refutation of the strong framing): (1) the Yagyū brand marker is the SURNAME, which is absent — the lord is "Kurosawa," the drillmaster surname-less; bare given names carry little Yagyū signal. (2) "Jūbei" is an extremely common Edo everyman tsūshō (≈ "Jack"), unremarkable for a foot-soldier. (3) They are NOT father-son in-game (lord's son is Naoyuki), so the "father-son collision" headline overstates it. (4) Character content actively contradicts the Yagyū figures — Munenori is non-martial/declining, Jūbei explicitly "competent-but-never-great"; the Hanzaki "one-eyed" compounding is coincidental (the drillmaster isn't one-eyed; Hanzaki's eye is a fog-terrain folklore device). So a knowledgeable player gets a mild "those are both Yagyū given names" eyebrow at most, not an obvious "the LLM copied Yagyū Munenori and his son."

Residual real gap worth surfacing: the specific co-occurrence of Munenori + Jūbei in one sword-training household, against a spec that machine-checks authenticity and fictionalised LESS-famous surnames (Toyama→Hayami, Konoe→Mukai), is a genuine denylist coverage hole the team never noticed.

Not trivially fixable: "Munenori" is the named apex granter threaded through the whole estate spine (~15+ refs in §2/§3/§5 + the succession arc) and Jūbei's creed is the tier's spine; and whether it's even a problem (common period given names vs. authenticity bar) is a taste judgment the human owns, plus a denylist-policy decision on extending guard to famous given names. Recommend a human authenticity glance: either accept as deliberate period-name scoping or rename one/both (renaming the lower-profile "Munenori" alone breaks the pair and is cheaper than touching Jūbei's spine creed).

confirmed.title: The 'Rank / Standing / Influence / level' naming thicket has no player-facing glossary or explainer
confirmed.detail: By the end of the first session a player is asked to hold apart at least eight near-synonymous progress concepts: character (combat) LEVEL, the Estate Service rung-METER, the Combat RANK rung-meter, the Arms PILLAR, the Estate & Wealth PILLAR, the Standing & Office PILLAR, House INFLUENCE (the 家威 roll-up), and the per-tier banzuke RANKING. Several collide by name: "Combat Rank" meter vs the "Arms" pillar vs combat "level"; "Estate Service" meter vs "Estate & Wealth" pillar; "Standing & Office" pillar vs the general notion of standing. Q9 already renamed "Combat Standing" -> "Combat Rank" specifically to reduce this collision, and §3.0 still warns that conflating the three combat tracks is "the single likeliest regression." If the spec authors keep tripping over it, a player will too. No in-game codex, glossary, or first-use "what these bars mean" surface is specified anywhere (tooltips per ui-design §5.9 explain individual locked items, but nothing explains the *relationship* among the bars).
confirmed.fix: HUMAN QUESTION: should v1 ship a single always-reachable "how the house rises" codex/legend (one screen mapping level vs rung-meter vs pillar vs Influence vs ranking), plus a first-time explainer the moment the second meter or the pillar panel reveals? Without it, the dense standing-vs-rank vocabulary is a real comprehension wall right after the protected first 30 min.
confirmed.verdict:
REAL, with one overstatement. Verified against the actual text: the PRD specifies NO player-facing glossary/codex/relationship-explainer anywhere (grep across prd.md, ui-design.md, fun-factor.md, qa-playtesting.md finds none), and §5.9 tooltips (ui-design.md:396-402) are explicitly per-ITEM (base info + a locked item's unlock condition) — they never explain the RELATIONSHIP among the bars. The naming thicket and collisions are real (§1.6.4, §2.16, §3.0.1, §2.18, §5.3): character LEVEL vs Combat RANK meter vs Arms PILLAR; Estate Service METER vs Estate & Wealth PILLAR; Standing & Office PILLAR vs generic "standing". The supporting evidence the claim cites checks out: Q9 renamed "Combat Standing"->"Combat Rank" specifically to reduce a collision (prd.md:611), and the spec repeatedly warns that conflating the three combat tracks is "the single likeliest regression" (lines 500, 2501, 3271, 5834) plus a kanji-homophone disambiguation (官威/官位, line 515) — strong evidence the naming is genuinely confusable.

Overstatement: "at least eight by the end of the first session" is too strong. Per prd.md:2637, in T0 the four-bar panel reveals ONLY Arms + Estate (Office reveals at T1, Name at T2), and reveals stagger one-at-a-time (NO-UI-DUMPS, lines 88/1155-1162) with distinct per-bar visual identity (kanji+emoji+colour, explicitly an anti-confusion measure, ui-design.md:340-342) and distance-to-next-gate on each bar. So the player never faces a wall of eight, and roughly six (not eight) progress concepts surface across T0's multi-hour floor.

But the core gap survives: those mitigations introduce/identify each bar individually; none explains cross-bar causality — exactly the question THREE combat-ish bars fed by three different combat-ish actions will provoke ("I'm fighting, why is my level rising but not Combat Rank, and neither is Arms?"). That is a genuine, legitimately-identified legibility/onboarding hole.

Not trivially fixable: this is a MISSING design surface, not a wording/number error. Whether to add a relationship-explainer, and how to do it without violating the project's own NO-UI-DUMPS / diegetic-reveal ethos (a literal codex modal risks reading as the very "AI-slop" the design forbids), is a human design decision. Severity medium: real and well-reasoned, and the spec itself keeps flagging the confusion, but bounded by the staggered-reveal + distinct-visual mitigations, the "eight by first session" overstatement, and the fact that this is exactly what playtest is meant to surface and close. Relevant files: /Users/raynos/projects/games/kami-kakushi/docs/prd.md (§1.6.4 ~580-625, §2.16 ~1997-2068, §3.0.1 ~2533-2588, §2.18 ~2169-2200, R7 reveal beat line 2637), /Users/raynos/projects/games/kami-kakushi/docs/ui-design.md (§5.3 ~333-354, §5.9 ~396-402).

confirmed.title: Import-over can be silently resurrected by a higher-counter autosave via the newest-wins selector
confirmed.where: §6.8.1 lines 6089-6098 (max saveCounter selector); §6.8 lines 6041-6044 ('Import validates the magic field + migrates'); §2.19(b) lines 2218-2222; §6.8.2 line 6122 (pre-overwrite snapshot)
confirmed.detail: Load picks the candidate with the highest monotonic `saveCounter` across all three backends. An imported save (an old export, a QA hand-off, a friend's file) carries its OWN counter — typically LOWER than this device's running autosave counter. The spec says import 'validates the magic field + migrates' and takes a pre-overwrite snapshot, but NEVER says import rebases the counter above the current max NOR that it overwrites all backends. So on the very next load the selector reads the still-present autosave (higher counter) and elects IT — silently discarding the import the player explicitly confirmed. The multi-backend redundancy makes this worse: a stale high-counter blob can survive on any one backend and win. The destructive-action confirm + snapshot protect the OLD data, not the imported data.
confirmed.fix: Specify that import rebases `saveCounter` to (max-seen-across-all-backends + 1) and atomically rewrites ALL available backends before returning. Question: does the import path do a counter-rebase-and-broadcast, or does it just load into memory and rely on the next autosave?
confirmed.verdict: REAL. The newest-wins selector elects the highest monotonic `saveCounter` across all backends (§2.19.1(b) L2258-2262; §6.8.1 L6089-6098; L6534), and `saveCounter` is carried inside the envelope and bumped per local save (L2275, L6046, L6091). The import path is specified only as "validates the magic field + migrates" (L6044) plus a destructive-action confirm and "pre-overwrite snapshot... before any overwrite" (L6063-6066, L6122). Exhaustive grep confirms the spec NEVER (a) rebases the imported envelope's counter above the local max (no rebase/restamp/max+1 anywhere), nor (b) mandates import write the imported blob to ALL backends, nor (c) provides read-repair/write-back-on-load (load is read-all/pick-newest with no healing write, L6534). The one downgrade guard at L6098 is about forward-`schemaVersion` fall-through, not counter regression — so it does not cover this. The monotonic counter is only sound within one device's save lineage; an imported file (old self-export, QA hand-off, friend's file) carries a foreign, typically-lower counter. Canonical case: export a backup, keep playing so the autosave counter climbs, then import the backup to roll back a bad state -> the backup's counter is below the surviving autosave's. Two reproducing paths: (1) the multi-backend-eviction edge the redundancy exists for (§2.19.1(d)) — a momentarily-evicted backend returns holding the old high-counter autosave and out-ranks the import; import is uniquely vulnerable because it is the only op that moves backwards in counter space; (2) if "import-over" loads into memory and relies on the next autosave, any reload/crash before that resurrects the all-backends old autosave. The confirm + snapshot protect the OLD data, not the import, exactly as claimed. This silently discards a player-confirmed import, violating the save layer's headline "no silent loss" guarantee (L6036-6037, L6043) on the primary recovery workflow — and the M0 DoD import test (export -> CLEAR store -> import, L6910/L6953) deliberately clears first, so it would pass while the bug stays latent. Not a wording fix: the remedy adds new save-layer contract (import rebases to global-max+1, writes to every backend, plus likely read-repair on load) and a new invariant + a DoD test that imports OVER a higher-counter autosave — design decisions touching D-013 / §6.8.1 / §6.8.2 / the M0 DoD that the human owns. Severity high (not critical) because a faithful "import-over -> make live -> save-to-all-available-backends with the next counter" implementation would close the common case, leaving the conditional eviction/no-immediate-write residue; but as the frozen spec is written, the invariant is genuinely unaddressed. Relevant: /Users/raynos/projects/games/kami-kakushi/docs/prd.md (§2.19 L2204-2249, §2.19.1 L2251-2276, §6.8 L6029-6074, §6.8.1 L6078-6100, §6.8.2 L6104-6125, M0 DoD L6534/6547/6550, §7.3 DoD L6910/6953).

otherDefects.title: End-of-M3 T0 is a playtest slice, NOT a shippable artifact — the entire deploy/polish/balance/legal/a11y spine is M6+M7
otherDefects.where: §7.2 M6 header + goal (lines 6811–6816), M7 (lines 6875–6880); §7.1.2 (balance is M6); §4.8.1 ('proposed' times); M1/M3 DoD (fun-proxies report-only, lines 6587, 6709)
otherDefects.detail: M0–M3 leaves T0 verify-green and playable, but a CLEAN ship needs work that is explicitly deferred: M6 = the balance pass that actually hits the LOCKED floor, the fun-proxy promotion from report-only to a GATING check, a11y acceptance (contrast/keyboard/screen-reader), inline-SVG + audio register; M7 = self-hosted OFL fonts, LICENSE, About/Credits, itch content descriptors, and the cross-origin-iframe save test. At M3-end the §4.8 numbers are still 'proposed', the pacing FLOOR is not yet regression-enforced, and the fun-proxies are report-only — so the slice is intentionally un-tuned and un-deployable. This is consistent with §7.4.2 ('no minimum shippable T0–T1 fallback was designed') — it is the answer to 'what blocks a clean T0 ship', not a flaw.
otherDefects.fix: Human question: is it worth carrying a thin 'playable-build-shareable-from-M3' checklist (self-host fonts + a stub LICENSE/Credits + the a11y-basics already in M0) so the M0–M3 vertical slice can be put in front of external playtesters, not just driven headlessly — given K3 wants 'a playable T0 vertical slice ASAP'?

otherDefects.title: T0 exercises the THINNEST version of the headline four-pillar system — only 2 of 4 bars ever light, no trade/combos/Office-steepening
otherDefects.where: §3.2 R7 row (line 2637), §4.8.1b T0 row (line 4385), §7.1.4 (T0 = 2 revealed pillars), §3.2 withheld note (lines 2639–2644)
otherDefects.detail: T0's Phase 2 reveals only Arms + Estate (the '2-pillar special'), with NO trade strand (the ≤⅓ cap is 'trivially satisfied', M3 DoD line 6695), NO cross-pillar combos (T2), and NO Office steepening (T1). The 4-bar Influence panel reveals at R7 with two bars permanently dark for the whole slice. As a playtest slice this is correct (test the simplest gate first) — but it means the marquee Influence economy, the trade-cap tension, and the anti-slump devices CANNOT be fun-validated until M4–M5, and a T0-only experience ends on a cliff ('Now: the valley.') into content the M0–M3 build does not contain. The system's interestingness is structurally back-loaded.
otherDefects.fix: Human question: accept that the four-pillar system's fun can only be partially read at the M0–M3 playtest (2 static bars), and that the first real Influence-economy feel signal comes at M4 — or pull a sliver of pillar-tension forward into T0 to de-risk the headline system earlier?

otherDefects.title: The scripted 'humbling first fight' vs. the 20–35%-win grindable wolf is conflated at the R3 anchor
otherDefects.detail: §5 narrates the FIRST wolf as a guaranteed loss ('thrashed, disarmed, ribs cracked, surviving only by luck, never by skill, never rescued'), and M2a phase-5 implements it as 'the scripted wolf-at-the-grain-store fight (survived by luck)'. But §4.8.1's R3 row attaches 'survive the wolf [20–35% win @ ≥0.7 satiety]' and §4.8.0 locks 'win-rate 20–35%', and the M2a DoD asserts a 20–35% win-rate band in a unit test. These are reconcilable (a soft-setback 'loss' IS survival, and the band applies to the post-drill-yard grindable wolves/sparring), but the copy invites a build ambiguity precisely at the M2a LOCKED anchor: is the first encounter a guaranteed-survival scripted beat or a real auto-resolved 20–35% fight?
otherDefects.fix: Trivial fix: state explicitly at §4.8.1 R3 / M2a phase-5 that the scripted first fight is a guaranteed-survival beat (win-or-soft-setback), and the LOCKED 20–35% win-rate is measured on the post-drill-yard GRINDABLE wolf/sparring encounter — not on the scripted humbling.

otherDefects.title: M3 carries disproportionate scope vs. the deliberately finely-split combat milestones — it is the most likely T0 slip
otherDefects.where: §7.2 M3 (lines 6667–6709) vs. the M2a/M2b split rationale (granularity note lines 6471–6473, R5 risk register line 7004)
otherDefects.detail: Combat got TWO fixed milestones (M2a/M2b, split up front at the R3→R4 seam) precisely because it is 'the densest stretch'. M3 then closes FOUR rungs (R4→R7), introduces TWO new top-level nav tabs (Crafting and Quests), two combat-reveal beats (stance at R5, ability/item at the L10 milestone), AND the entire four-pillar Phase-2 accrual engine + hybrid gate + House screen + ranking read + first shinden + E1 and E2 builds. The Influence economy makes its first real appearance here, with the most-asserted invariants of the whole slice (deeds-only-in-Phase-2, trade ≤⅓, jumps+seasonal-only, gate-against-revealed-pillars-only). It is sliceable rung-by-rung, but it is the densest single milestone in T0 and the one whose slip would push out the whole vertical slice.
otherDefects.fix: Opportunity: consider pre-splitting M3 at the R6→R7 seam — Phase-1 finish (R4–R6: Crafting tab, Quests tab, stance/ability slots, E1/E2, shinden basis) vs. Phase-2 open (R7: the four-pillar accrual engine + hybrid gate + House screen) — the same up-front-seam discipline that made M2a/M2b two safe checkpoints, so the Influence-economy build is isolated from the four-rung content build.

otherDefects.title: The first ~60–65 min is labour-only; combat is 'early' only relative to a 28.5h saga
otherDefects.detail: R0→R2 (cold open + ~60 min of rake-rice / forage / woodcut / haul) is pure peaceful labour before any fight; the humbling first fight is ~60–75 min in. The PRD repeatedly frames combat as 'EARLY (T0-R3),' which is true against the full saga — but in absolute first-session terms it is an hour of single-texture labour before the genre's biggest hook (combat) appears. The thinnest single stretch is R1: it reveals the labour loop + paddies + world-clock at its START, but the Skills tab does not reveal until R2 — so for the entire ~30-min R1 grind the player has no skill-progress UI, no combat, and no new nav, leaving only the koku counter and the (possibly invisible) rung-meter as feedback. That is the likeliest 'flat air' window in the first hour, sitting right after the rapid-fire cold open creates a momentum cliff.
otherDefects.fix: Consider surfacing the Skills tab (or a single mid-R1 micro-reveal — first XP pop, a new resource row, a one-line foreshadow of the wolf) inside R1 so the first 30-min grind visibly accrues something beyond koku. Question for the human: is ~60 min of labour-only on-ramp the intended slow-burn, or should the first skirmish/threat foreshadow land before R3 to bridge the gap?

otherDefects.title: Early combat is thin on active decisions — ability/item interventions arrive after the 2-hour mark
otherDefects.detail: The combat-reveal ladder gives R3 the bare auto-resolve loop + retreat, R4 durability bands, R5 the stance slot — but the ability + item intervention slots unlock only at the first weapon-line L10 milestone, which (starting from weaponSkill 0 at R3) lands past the ~130-min mark covered by the first 2 hours. So for the first hour-plus of combat the active-decision space is just: which mob, eat-before-fight, when to retreat, and gear — most of it pre-fight setup, with little mid-fight agency. The project's own bar (fun-factor §1: active-only is justified only if 'every active minute offers a decision, not a wait') is most at risk precisely here, where a player drawn in by the R3 fight then watches several auto-resolves with thin intervention. This is the window where a combat-motivated player decides whether the combat track has depth.
otherDefects.fix: Consider pulling ONE intervention earlier (e.g., the item/consumable slot at R4 alongside crafting, or a basic stance choice that visibly swings outcomes at R3) so the first hour of combat has at least one live mid-fight decision. Question: is the L10-milestone gate on abilities/items tuned to land inside T0's combat window, or does it deliberately sit past the first session?

otherDefects.title: Two of the seven core fun loops never fire in the first 2 hours — the pillar-climb and seasonal-exam pleasures are absent
otherDefects.detail: House Influence (the four pillars) does not reveal until R7 (~4.5h in, Phase-2 entry), and pillar DEEDS plus seasonal judged results accrue only in Phase 2. So the 'watch a meaningful number climb' loop (fun-factor §2.3) and the 'seasonal exam anticipation-and-payoff' loop (§2.5) — arguably the two signature hooks of the incremental genre — are entirely absent from the make-or-break first session. The first 2h rests on four loops: deed (koku/XP), rung-climb, reveal, and combat-tension. That is defensible (the pillars are the Phase-2 payoff and revealing them early would clutter the lean opening), and lesser climbing numbers do exist (koku, character level, skill levels, the rung-meter). But it means the genre's most addictive 'standing that means something, judged on a clock' sensation is deferred past the window where most players decide to stay.
otherDefects.fix: Confirm the deed/rung/reveal/combat quartet is genuinely strong enough to carry 2h without any pillar or seasonal-appraisal beat. Opportunity: a lightweight early proxy (e.g., the domain-ranking read teased as 'unranked,' or one anticipatory seasonal-harvest beat in T0) could pre-echo the seasonal-exam loop before R7. Question for the human: is deferring both number-go-up-that-means-something AND the seasonal exam entirely out of the first session an accepted risk?

otherDefects.title: The AND-gate 'awaiting X' state risks a goal-gradient stall at the worst moment — a promotion that won't fire
otherDefects.detail: Each rung promotes on an AND-gate: rung-meter ≥ threshold AND story flags satisfied, with the UI reading 'awaiting X' when one side lags. In the first 2h this is the entire promotion engine. The fun risk: a player who races the curated activities maxes the meter but then sits at 'awaiting' a story beat — at exactly the goal-gradient peak (meter full, next title in sight) the climb can stall into confusion if 'awaiting X' does not also tell the player HOW to satisfy X. An unactionable 'awaiting' state turns the most motivating moment (a promotion about to land) into an anti-climax/dead-end, which is the opposite of the 'one more rung' pull the design is built on.
otherDefects.fix: Specify that 'awaiting X' names a concrete, navigable next action (the activity, NPC, or place that sets the flag), not just the unmet condition. Question for the human: is the 'awaiting X' copy guaranteed to be actionable (a pointer to what to do), or can a player be left with a full meter and no clear path to the story flag?

otherDefects.title: Goal-gradient fun depends on a visibly filling rung-meter, but the spec only commits to 'awaiting X' text
otherDefects.detail: The 'one more rung' loop (fun-factor §2.2) explicitly relies on 'a partly-filled meter' and the goal-gradient effect — motivation rising as a visible goal nears. In the lean R1/R2 labour stretches the per-rung Estate Service / Combat Rank meter is the PRIMARY moment-to-moment feedback (koku aside), so whether it is surfaced as a filling bar with the next title labelled is load-bearing for first-hour momentum. The PRD defines the RungMeter data shape and the 'awaiting X' lag text, but does not explicitly promise a continuously-visible filling bar; thresholds are deliberately modest (~14–19), which is fine numerically but means the visual fill, not the raw number, must carry the goal-gradient. If the meter is only implied (or only shows when one side lags), the leanest stretches lose their main feedback signal.
otherDefects.fix: Confirm the active rung-meter renders as a labelled, continuously-filling progress bar ('Estate Service → Bonded hand') from R1 onward, not only as 'awaiting X' on a lag. Trivial to specify in §2.1/§2.15.1 if intended; worth an explicit line given how much first-hour pacing leans on it.

otherDefects.title: Fragile elegance: the three-track separation rests on per-activity authoring discipline — the same in-world act naturally feeds all three, and the verifier catches sum-leakage, not intent-drift
otherDefects.where: §2.8.1 (L1556 'single likeliest regression'); §4.1.1 (L3387-3389 'authored SEPARATELY from the pillar-deed inventory'); §6.6.1 no-leakage (L5954-5956)
otherDefects.detail: The PRD twice names re-conflating the three tracks as 'the single likeliest regression,' which is itself an admission that the elegance is fragile. The cleanliness depends on content authors correctly deciding, per activity, which of the three streams it feeds — but a single fictional act like 'clear the bandit nest' plausibly SHOULD feed all three (kill→XP, recognised deed→Arms in Phase 2, curated activity→Combat Rank meter in Phase 1). The verifier asserts the numeric streams sum independently (no field cross-writes), but it cannot assert that the authored TAGGING matches design intent — an activity mis-tagged into two legitimate streams, or a 'curated activity' that is really a disguised kill-counter, passes the sum check while quietly eroding the separation the whole architecture exists to protect. The separation is conceptually airtight but authoring-load-bearing.
otherDefects.fix: Consider a verifier rule (or authoring lint) on the activity/deed registries that flags any single content id wired into more than one of {combatXp source, Arms deed, rungActivityTag}, forcing a deliberate per-case override — turning the 'separately authored' convention into a checked invariant rather than authorial discipline.

otherDefects.title: Fragile elegance: the rung-meter ≡ floor identity assumes eligibleActivityRate is a true ceiling, but bounded perks (D-027) + auto-repeat (FU23) can exceed the assumed rate and silently breach the floor
otherDefects.where: §4.1.1 claim (L3418-3420 'still cannot beat the ~30-min floor, because the rate ceiling is what the floor is computed against'); cross-pressures: D-027 per-skill perks, FU23 auto-repeat (L4311), §4.8 undershoot-fail gate (L4312)
otherDefects.detail: The 'same constraint two ways' elegance (finding 2) holds only if `eligibleActivityRate(rung)` is a genuine upper bound on how fast a player can complete that rung's curated activities. But the V2 design added two things that push completion rate upward and are not obviously bounded into that rate estimate: bounded per-skill combat perks (D-027, 'no global cap') and tab-open auto-repeat labour (FU23). If a well-built or efficiently-looped player clears the curated set faster than the authored rate, the meter fills before 30 min and the floor is breached — yet the §4.8 gate only fails on UNDERSHOOT, so it would CATCH this, but the §4.1.1 narrative ('cannot beat the floor') currently reads as a guarantee rather than a tuning assumption. The elegance is real but its 'cannot' is contingent on the rate model staying conservative as perks/gear/auto-loops compound.
otherDefects.fix: Reword §4.1.1 to state the floor is enforced by the undershoot gate against the WORST-CASE (fastest-achievable) activity rate including perks/gear/auto-repeat — and have the verifier back-solve thresholds from a max-rate estimate, not the 'intended pace' rate, so the floor is guaranteed rather than nominal.

otherDefects.title: Name per-event caps in the deed table don't equal 0.04·good-band, and Name's T2 deed bases exceed the §4.1-derived cap (violating the stated 'every base ≤ its cap')
otherDefects.where: §4.2.1 (L3495-3496 Name rows: cap column 'T0 40 · T1 400 · T2 3.2K', bases 1.6K & 1.8K at T2) vs the cap rule (L3465/L3468 cap=0.04·goodBand) vs §4.1 (L3346 Name T2 good=28K)
otherDefects.detail: The cap rule is cap=0.04·good-band. §4.1 gives Name T2 good=28K, so the cap must be 0.04·28K=1.12K. But §4.2.1 states the Name T2 cap as 3.2K (which implies a stale good band of 80K; the Name caps 40/400/3.2K imply 1K/10K/80K, neither matching §4.1's re-derived 28K nor TIER_REF_NAME=140K). Consequently Name's two T2 deed bases (1.6K and 1.8K) BOTH exceed the §4.1-correct cap of 1.12K, so they would be silently clamped — directly contradicting §4.2.1 L3473 'Every deed base in the table sits at or under its own pillar/tier cap … the cap virtually never silently clamps a deed.' The worked cap examples at L3472 conspicuously list Arms/Estate/Office but omit Name, suggesting Name was never reconciled to the §4.1 band re-derivation.
otherDefects.fix: Recompute the Name cap column from §4.1 (T2 = 0.04·28K = 1.12K) and re-tune the Name deed bases (1.6K/1.8K) down under that cap, or correct §4.1's Name bands if 80K was intended. Same root cause as the Name-seasonal defect: Name's reference magnitudes are stale relative to the §4.1 re-derivation.

otherDefects.title: §4.1.1 says the rung-meter 'threshold rises gently' but its own formula and table show the threshold FALLING
otherDefects.where: §4.1.1 (L3396 'thresholdForRung = 30·eligibleActivityRate'; L3403-3404 'the activity rate drops slightly at higher rungs … so the threshold rises gently'; threshold table L3408-3416 showing R1→R7 = 18,19,17,17,17,15,14)
otherDefects.detail: Threshold = 30·rate, and the prose states the rate DROPS at higher rungs (rate column 0.60→0.46). A falling rate times a fixed 30 makes the threshold FALL, which is exactly what the table shows (18 down to 14). So 'the threshold rises gently' is the inverse of both the stated formula and the printed numbers. (The thing that escalates across rungs is the wall-clock — 30→55 min in §4.8.1 — but that escalation comes from the story/build AND-gate, not the meter threshold.) This also softly undercuts L3401's 'the meter and the time floor are the same constraint expressed two ways': for R4–R7 the meter fills in 30 min while the rung takes 35–55 min, so the meter is not the binding floor there.
otherDefects.fix: Change 'the threshold rises gently' to 'the threshold falls gently (fewer activity-points fill the same ≥30-min floor as each activity is worth less per minute)'. Trivial wording fix; numbers are otherwise self-consistent.

otherDefects.title: The 2nd T0 weapon's reveal rung conflicts between the milestone map (R4) and the roster-growth ladder (R6)
otherDefects.where: §7.2.0 (L6486: 'R4 (loot→craft loop) … + a found/crafted 2nd T0 weapon | M2b') vs §3.5.1(b) (L2853: 'T0-R6 + a 2nd T0 beat | +2 more (completes Line 1 by end-T0)')
otherDefects.detail: The two tables that pin a rung for the extra T0 weapons disagree: §7.2.0 lands the 2nd T0 weapon at R4 (milestone M2b), while §3.5.1(b)'s roster-growth table lands the '+2 more' at R6 (+a 2nd beat). M2b covers the R3→R4 seam and M3 covers R4→R7, so this also splits the weapon across two milestones. §4.6.9's canonical roster table (L4138-4139) sidesteps the conflict by tagging both extra spears only as 'T0' (no rung), and §2.10.1(e) L1694 says only '+2 across T0' — so neither canonical source resolves which of R4/R6 is correct.
otherDefects.fix: Pin a single rung for weapon #2 (e.g. R4 found/crafted via the loot loop, weapon #3 at R6) and make §3.5.1(b), §7.2.0, and §4.6.9 agree. Low-effort once the human/owner picks the rung.

otherDefects.title: The universal hybrid-gate predicate ('good in ALL · great in 2-3 · excellent in 1-2') is contradicted by the T1 profile, which requires no excellent
otherDefects.where: Predicate stated verbatim in §1.6.3 (L544), §1.6.4 (L617), §2.16 (L2034 + TierGate L2079) and §4.1 (L3314) vs the T1 row (§1.6.3 L565 / §2.16 L2050 / §4.1 L3340 / §4.8.1b L4386): 'good in all 3 + great in 2', no excellent
otherDefects.detail: The gate is defined four times as the conjunction 'good in ALL revealed · great in 2–3 · excellent in 1–2'. T0 is explicitly carved out as the 2-pillar special case (good in 2 + excellent in 1, no great). But T1 is NOT carved out, yet its authored profile is 'good in 3 + great in 2' with zero excellent — violating the 'excellent in 1–2' clause of the very predicate the TierGate.distributionPredicate (§2.16 L2079) claims to enforce. So two of the three shipping tiers (T0, T1) fail to satisfy the universal triple as written.
otherDefects.fix: State that the great/excellent COUNTS are per-tier levers that may be 0 (§4.1 L3373 already treats the counts as levers), and reword the universal predicate to 'good in ALL revealed; great/excellent counts vary per tier (T0: 0 great/1 excellent; T1: 2 great/0 excellent; T2: …)'. Spec-clarity fix, not a balance change.

otherDefects.title: Q9 'macronize rōnin' is half-applied, and the §6.6 macron-lint contradicts itself on it
otherDefects.where: Q9 (Block L); §6.6 macron lint L5918–5921; un-macronized prose at L1626, 2761, 2923, 5875, 6376, 6764, 6804
otherDefects.detail: Q9 explicitly says 'macronize gōshi/rōnin'. gōshi is fully macronized everywhere, and rōnin is macronized in 12 narrative spots — but 'ronin' is left un-macronized in 8 mostly-bestiary/mob-list spots (e.g. 'boars/wolves/monkeys/bandits/ronin/smugglers'). Worse, the §6.6 macron-lint spec is internally contradictory: line 5919 lists '*rōnin*' in the macron-REQUIRED examples, while line 5921 puts 'ronin' in the naturalized-exonym ALLOW-LIST that is 'exempt and may stay in their common form.' As written, the lint would bless the un-macronized form and so could never enforce the Q9 fix.
otherDefects.fix: Pick one: remove 'ronin' from the §6.6 allow-list and macronize the 8 prose occurrences to 'rōnin' (honours Q9), OR ask the human to re-confirm rōnin as a naturalized exonym (which would reverse Q9 and needs an explicit newest-wins note). The lint must not list rōnin as both required and exempt.

otherDefects.title: Stored settings surface (§6.4) omits persisted a11y/audio fields that §2.21 declares persisted (Q18/Q50/D16)
otherDefects.where: §6.4 GameState.settings (L5790) vs §2.21(c) A11ySettings/AudioSettings (L2359–2361)
otherDefects.detail: §2.21(c) declares 'A11ySettings { textScale, colorblindMode, reduceMotion, paused, liveRegionScope } (persisted)' and 'AudioSettings { ambientVolume, sfxVolume, muted }'. But the §6.4 persisted GameState only stores 'settings: { textScale, colourblindMode, reducedMotion, muted }' — missing the audio volume controls (ambientVolume/sfxVolume) and liveRegionScope (and paused). Q50's 'good audio' implies real volume controls and D16/Q18 imply persisted a11y prefs; if §6.4 is taken as the storage contract, those human-signed settings would not persist across reload.
otherDefects.fix: Trivial: extend §6.4 GameState.settings to include ambientVolume/sfxVolume (and liveRegionScope if it is ever non-fixed), OR add a one-line note that §6.4's settings is illustrative and the full A11ySettings/AudioSettings of §2.21(c) is the persisted shape.

otherDefects.title: Q43 is unaccounted-for in both the binding feedback doc and the PRD
otherDefects.where: prd_human_feedback.md Block L header 'all 56 decisions (Q1–Q56)' (L388) vs body; grep 'Q43' = 0 hits in prd.md
otherDefects.detail: Block L's header claims it resolves 'all 56 decisions (Q1–Q56)', but the enumerated body has no Q43 entry, and 'Q43' appears nowhere in prd.md either. (By contrast Q25/Q40 are explicitly folded into Q5, and Q49 — though also absent from the Block L list — does appear in the PRD via the Q19/Q49 a11y pairing.) Because Q43 has no home in the binding intent record, its disposition cannot be audited against the PRD — it could be a genuinely dropped decision or a numbering artifact.
otherDefects.fix: Human/maintainer: confirm whether Q43 exists in the decisions master sheet (brainstorms/2026-06-26-prd-decisions-master.md) and either fold it into the feedback doc with its landing spot, or annotate it as intentionally void so '56' ties out.

otherDefects.title: Budget-as-a-FLOOR + labour-perk skillCombatBonus can breach the LOCKED 20–35% first-fight win-rate
otherDefects.where: §4.5.4 perks no global cap (3898–3906), §4.5.3 L10 labour combat perk (3865), §4.6.6 'not yet milled out at R3' (4079–4081), §4.6.1 attackPower formula (3949)
otherDefects.detail: Per-skill perks add skillCombatBonus directly to attackPower (3949) with NO hard global cap (3898), unlocked by leveling ANY skill including labour (L10 'small combat perk', 3865). R1–R2 are pure labour rungs and the budget is an explicit FLOOR ('the saga can and should run longer', overshoot is permitted, 4307–4314), so a labour-first player can grind R1–R2 indefinitely, mill L10+ on several labour skills, and enter R3 with skillCombatBonus already > 0. At R3 base attackPower ≈ 9 (weaponBase 3 + 1.2·STR 5); stacking even +4 to +6 skillCombatBonus is +45–65% attackPower — enough to push the wolf win-rate above the LOCKED 20–35% anchor. The PRD's stated assumption that perks are 'not yet milled out at R3' (4080–4081) holds only for an on-pace player, NOT the grind-loving profile the FLOOR explicitly courts, and the §4.6.7 win-rate harness measures a fixed reference build so CI won't catch it.
otherDefects.fix: Gate skillCombatBonus-bearing perks behind a combat-track unlock (post-R3) so labour leveling can't front-load combat power, or cap pre-R3 labour-skill levels. Human Q: should an over-grinding labour-first player be allowed to walk into the locked humbling first fight already strong?

otherDefects.title: The signature silk meibutsu is a trade-≤⅓ trap, and the Estate×Name 'celebrated meibutsu' combo is diegetically trade but must be credited off-strand to preserve the cap
otherDefects.where: §4.2.3 trade clamp (3667–3695), §4.3.1 combo pairs/rules (3764–3777), §4.7.1 sericulture (4209)
otherDefects.detail: The trade strand is hard-clamped to ≤⅓ of Estate & Wealth, and the LOCKED signature economic activity (silk/sericulture meibutsu) 'lives entirely inside this capped strand' (3687–3688). A player who leans into the game's marquee trade content will silently hit the clamp (trade = 0.5·(land+treasury)); every silk deed beyond that is wasted with no described in-fiction signal — a feels-bad newbie trap on the most thematic activity. Separately, §4.3.1 lists an 'Estate × Name (a celebrated meibutsu)' combo (3774): meibutsu IS silk = the TRADE strand, yet rule 1 (3763–3764) credits the combo's Estate component to the 'already-clamped Estate total' to preserve ⅓. That only holds if the combo's Estate side is routed to LAND/TREASURY rather than TRADE — an unstated routing that contradicts the deed's own fiction (a famed silk product crediting non-trade Estate).
otherDefects.fix: Add an explicit in-fiction 'trade saturated' signal when the ⅓ clamp binds, and state that the Estate×Name combo's Estate side routes to LAND/TREASURY (not the trade strand) — or drop the meibutsu pairing to avoid the routing contradiction.

otherDefects.title: Cross-pillar combos are excluded from the gate-check, so they provide zero acceleration toward tier-up — undercutting their stated anti-slump purpose
otherDefects.detail: Combos are sold as the 'T2 anti-slump device' that smooths the grind. But rule 3 (3767–3771) makes combo-sourced ip 'not counted' in the §4.1 gate-threshold evaluation. Since tier-up depends ONLY on the gate-relevant pillar totals, combos do not reduce time-to-gate at all — they add ip that is invisible to the very thing the player is grinding toward. The implementation also now requires tracking two totals per pillar (gate-relevant vs display), adding complexity for a device that, by construction, cannot accelerate progression. The anti-slump benefit is therefore purely cosmetic (bigger numbers flashing), not a real pacing relief.
otherDefects.fix: Decide the intent: either let combos count toward the gate (real anti-slump acceleration, re-tie the 70/30 budget around it) or relabel them as flavor-only number-flash so the spec doesn't imply a pacing benefit they cannot deliver.

otherDefects.title: M3 is a milestone that secretly contains three-to-four milestones (the entire Influence economy, unsplit)
otherDefects.where: §7.2 M3 (lines ~6667-6709); contrast §7.2 M2a/M2b split & granularity note (~6466-6476)
otherDefects.detail: Combat — the stretch the PRD itself calls 'the densest' — got a planned two-milestone split (M2a/M2b at the R3→R4 seam). M3 got none, yet it lands: the ENTIRE four-pillar Phase-2 accrual engine (the game's central economy — achievement jumps + per-event caps + seasonal judged-result-on-high-water-mark + the trade≤⅓ clamp + recoverable dents + the HYBRID good/great/excellent gate) PLUS the Quest system and its top-level tab with 4 quest types PLUS the Crafting top-level tab PLUS four rungs R4-R7 PLUS the R5 stance + L10 ability/item combat reveals PLUS the House screen + domain-ranking read + the two-bar Influence panel. That is the Influence-economy milestone, the quest milestone, and the crafting-tab milestone fused with a 4-rung content slice. Phase-task 1 alone ('build the four-pillar Phase-2 accrual engine + tests') is a milestone-sized deliverable. M3 is the single highest-risk schedule item and the most likely to stall the roadmap.
otherDefects.fix: Split M3 at the R6→R7 (Phase-1→Phase-2) seam the way combat was split: M3a = R4-R6 quests+crafting-tab+combat-reveal content on the existing engines; M3b = the R7 capstone that OPENS Phase-2 + the whole four-pillar accrual engine + the hybrid gate. The Influence economy deserves at least the same fixed-split treatment combat got.

otherDefects.title: M0 front-loads the entire persistence engine (highest bug-density subsystem) against a schema too trivial to exercise it
otherDefects.where: §7.2 M0 'Lands'/'DoD'/tasks 5-8 (lines ~6526-6550); §7.1.1 Save row; cross-iframe survival deferred to M7
otherDefects.detail: M0 ('toolchain bootstrap + cold open') also commits the FULL multi-backend redundant save: atomic write-to-all of IndexedDB+localStorage+sessionStorage, app-identity magic field, monotonic-counter newest-wins arbitration, an ordered additive-schema MIGRATION chain + pre-migration raw backup, base64 export/import — simultaneously with the toolchain, the pure-core boundary, the per-stream-cursor RNG, the reveal/reward bus, the content-verifier, gen:docs, and the DEV API. That is 3-4 foundation milestones in one. The migration chain has nothing to migrate (M0's stored surface is ~{schemaVersion, rng, clock, character{hp,satiety,attributePoints,level}}); the multi-backend arbitration's only real failure mode (the cross-origin-iframe storage partition) is explicitly deferred to M7. So the most bug-prone subsystem is built first against a trivial schema, and the test that would actually validate it is 7 milestones away.
otherDefects.fix: Keep the save ABSTRACTION + base64 round-trip + magic-field in M0 (cheap insurance), but defer the migration-chain machinery and multi-backend newest-wins arbitration to the first milestone that adds a second schema version / non-trivial state (M1). At minimum, justify why the iframe-survival validation can be deferred while the engine it validates ships in M0.

otherDefects.title: M4 and M5 each bundle several net-new ENGINES under a single 'content tier' label, with some rates deferred
otherDefects.where: §7.2 M4 Lands+tasks 3&4 (~6711-6753); §7.2 M5 Lands+8 tasks (~6755-6809); §4.7.2 '(deferred) coin/saturation-recovery'
otherDefects.detail: 'Complete a tier' reads like content authoring but each tier is multiple engines: M4 = component/quality crafting engine + the silk TRADE sub-engine (MarketState{perGoodPriceIndex, saturationByGood, recoveryRate} with a per-unit progressive saturation damper — and its coin spread/sink/saturation-recovery NUMBERS are explicitly deferred per §4.7.2, so M4 must both build AND first-balance a new economy) + the static multi-node reputation web + intra-line dialogue branching (choices[]/ChoiceId persistence) + the 2nd combat line. M5 = the region trade backbone + the sekisho travel-standing layer + the Origin faction + O0-O5 ladder + the cross-pillar combo engine + the 3rd combat line + E3 + the Daikan stub (8 phase-tasks). Rung-by-rung sub-slicing mitigates the content, not the engines — each engine is its own design/test/balance surface.
otherDefects.fix: In §7.2, call out the net-new ENGINES inside M4/M5 (silk-market, rep-web, intra-line-dialogue; sekisho, cross-pillar-combos) as named sub-slices with their own verify-green DoD, distinct from the rung content, so the schedule reflects that 'finish T1/T2' is not just authoring. Pin or explicitly park the deferred silk-market rates before M4 starts, since the trade≤⅓ invariant and the cross-pillar combos both depend on that engine.

otherDefects.title: The QA harness cannot deliver the guarantees it is specified to provide (optimal-floor proof, time-compression, dead-time)
otherDefects.where: qa-playtesting.md §1 (toRung/toTier 'play the optimal/scripted path') & §2 ('optimal-ish' bot validates the floor) & §3 (no-dead-time); §6.10 (forceState + toRung/toTier coexist); §7.2 M6 task 3
otherDefects.detail: Three harness claims are not buildable as written. (1) The undershoot gate must prove NO player clears a grind rung in <28 min, but the bot is a 'simple greedy/heuristic' (qa §2) — a heuristic that takes 30 min does not prove a cleverer strategy can't take 10; floor robustness is asserted but unprovable by this harness (it actually rests on the unstated per-activity-cost invariant in finding #1). (2) `toRung(id)`/`toTier(n)` are specified as 'fast-forward by PLAYING the optimal/scripted path' — that is the auto-player itself (built far later) or a hand-maintained scripted playthrough that must be re-authored every time content changes; §6.10 also offers `forceState`, but a forceState jump skips the very play a pacing run needs to measure. (3) 'No dead-time: no meaningful action and no reward incoming' is a human-experiential metric; a bot that always picks the best legal action instantly never experiences a wait, so the proxy can only ever be a derived inter-reward tick-gap, not the thing the fun doc describes.
otherDefects.fix: Re-specify: (1) enforce the floor by a strategy-independent per-activity game-clock cost (then a heuristic bot suffices, because no strategy can beat it) and say so; (2) define toRung/toTier as deterministic forceState JUMPS for setup and forbid measuring pacing through them — pacing is measured only on a full played run; (3) redefine 'dead-time' as a concrete max inter-reward tick-gap on the played path, with the threshold pinned (it's currently '~N s', an open tunable in fun-factor.md §8).

otherDefects.title: The central tier-up gate predicate is unimplementable at the milestone that ships it — counts are ranges deferred to M6
otherDefects.where: §4.1 ('great in 2-3 · excellent in 1-2'; 'plus the great/excellent COUNTS' as a lever; lines ~3314,3372); §4.9 open-questions ('the great/excellent counts… PROVISIONAL, tuned at M6'); §7.2 M3/M4/M5 build the gate
otherDefects.detail: The HYBRID gate is the thing that ends every tier, and it is BUILT at M3 (T0), M4 (T1), M5 (T2). But the exact pass counts are specified as RANGES ('great in 2-3', 'excellent in 1-2') and explicitly listed as a lever/open-question tuned at M6 — after all three tiers are built. An engineer coding the tier-up predicate at M3 cannot write it: for T1's 3 revealed pillars, 'good in all 3 + great in 2-3 + excellent in 1-2' admits multiple non-equivalent predicates. Nesting semantics (does a pillar at the excellent band also satisfy a 'great' slot and a 'good' slot? the band magnitudes imply yes, but it's never stated) are left to the reader. And the §6.6.1 'accrual tie-out' can only tie the deed inventory to the GOOD band — it cannot tie out unfixed great/excellent counts, so that verifier check is partial until M6.
otherDefects.fix: Pin the exact counts per tier (e.g. T1 = good×3 + great×2 + excellent×1, disjoint or nested — state which) BEFORE M3 codes the predicate, even as provisional-but-concrete numbers; ranges and 'tune at M6' are fine for magnitudes but not for the count logic that determines the predicate's shape. Explicitly state the band-nesting rule (great ⇒ good).

otherDefects.title: Incomplete rōnin macron sweep (Q9 violated) — bare 'ronin' persists across the data/tech/bestiary layers, including the lint's own allow-list
otherDefects.detail: Q9 (Block L) and the §1.10 lock (line 1006) explicitly require 'Macronize gōshi and rōnin forms project-wide.' gōshi is clean (zero 'goshi' hits), but rōnin is half-done: the narrative/cast prose writes 'rōnin' while every systems/tech/bestiary table writes bare 'ronin' (enemies.ts content spec, §7 bestiary row, the M-build tasks). Worst: line 5921 is the §6.6 REAL-NAME romanization lint's own allow-list — it lists 'ronin' un-macroned in the same breath as 'daimyō (written with its macron)', so the mechanism meant to enforce romanization codifies the wrong form. The macron sweep touched the human-facing prose but never reached the data layer that generates the shipped strings.
otherDefects.fix: Replace bare 'ronin' → 'rōnin' in §2.9/§3.6/§6.5/§6.6/§7, and have the §6.6 lint actively flag un-macroned 'ronin' (and 'goshi'/'kaido'/'sekisho' etc.) rather than allow-listing it. Trivial find-replace plus one lint rule.

otherDefects.title: Stray pre-rename name 'Obaa Sato' leaked into a live-reference spot (§7 parked list)
otherDefects.where: §7 parked-items table (line 6411) vs the correct live name in §5 T3.7 (5371) and §1.8 (720)
otherDefects.detail: Line 6411 reads 'keep Ranpo / Obaa Sato as seeds only' — but Obaa Sato was RENAMED to 'Obaa Kuni' (Q39, off the Sayo near-rhyme); §5 T3.7 line 5371 correctly says 'keep Ranpo / Obaa Kuni as the existing seed only.' Every other 'Obaa Sato' occurrence is in legitimate rename-tracking context (the §1.10 note, the §6.6 lint description); this one is a live reference to the character by her dead name. The Q39 denylist lint that the doc says guards 'Obaa Sato kept non-colliding' (line 5969) should itself flag this exact string, so it doubles as a self-test the lint would fail.
otherDefects.fix: Change §7 line 6411 'Obaa Sato' → 'Obaa Kuni'. Confirm the §6.6 lint denylists the literal old strings (Obaa Sato / Naozane / Mago / Kuranosuke) so future leaks are caught.

otherDefects.title: Emoji-as-period-motif undercuts the woodblock register and OS-consistency the bible itself demands
otherDefects.where: ui-design.md §7 locked emoji set (lines 457–466) + §2/§5.3 pillar emoji (81–85, 344–350)
otherDefects.detail: The UI bible leans on emoji for load-bearing-adjacent concepts: 🏯 castle/house, ⚔️/🗡️ Arms, 🪙 coin/mon, plus the pillar identities. Several render as unmistakably modern 'sticker' glyphs (🏯 is a stylized modern castle; ⚔️ crossed-swords is generic-gamey; 🪙 a cartoon coin) and — the bible's own §4.2/§4.6 rationale for choosing inline SVG is 'consistent across OSes' — emoji render very differently per platform, the exact cross-OS inconsistency the woodblock handmade look can't absorb. The bible acknowledges the slop risk and mitigates (CSS desaturate filter §7, aria-hidden, Q38 moves 'load-bearing period motifs' to inline SVG / 'emoji cosmetic-only'), so this is managed — but the most modern-rendering glyphs (🏯, ⚔️, 🪙) still sit in accent roles on the hero surfaces (nav, log bullets, pillar panel) where a single Apple-vs-Android-vs-Windows render swing reads as the AI-slop the §9 checklist bans.
otherDefects.fix: Migrate the few worst offenders (🏯 castle, ⚔️ Arms, 🪙 coin) from emoji to the existing inline-SVG mon/motif system so the woodblock surface carries zero OS-variable glyphs; keep emoji only for genuinely period-safe, low-stakes tags (🌾 🍶 🏮 🌸 🍁). Human call on where the emoji/SVG line sits.

otherDefects.title: The load-bearing 'rigged box' motif mis-models Edo measure-fraud (a doctored kyō-masu)
otherDefects.where: §1.8 antagonist table (850, 856); §5 T1.3 Magobei (4854-area); §5 T3.3 Kuroiwa (5324)
otherDefects.detail: The recurring [MOTIF: rigged box] through-line (Magobei T1 → Kuroiwa T3 → Echizen-ya T4) is a 'doctored kyō-masu.' But the kyō-masu (京枡) was specifically the bakufu's STANDARDIZED, anti-fraud official measure — its whole purpose was to stop measure-cheating. Classic Edo measure-fraud was instead the two-masu trick (a larger iri-masu to take rice in, a smaller dashi-masu to pay out) or a false-bottomed/short masu, not a tampered 'standard' box. Calling the rigged object itself a kyō-masu slightly muddles the period mechanic of a motif that recurs across three tiers and is meant to reward the curious player.
otherDefects.fix: Specify the fraud as a two-masu swap or a false-bottomed/short masu (and reserve 'kyō-masu' for the legitimate standard it counterfeits). Deepens authenticity of a load-bearing through-line at near-zero cost; human can confirm the exact period mechanic to dramatize.

otherDefects.title: Pace is locked to a wall-clock the player cannot accelerate — the core idle/incremental retention loop (optimize → go faster) is designed OUT
otherDefects.detail: Three mechanisms together remove almost all player agency over pace: (a) the rung-meter threshold is explicitly back-solved so that 'a player who races the curated activities still cannot beat the ~30-min floor, because the rate ceiling is what the floor is computed against' (§4.1.1 L3418-3420) — i.e. max efficiency and minimum efficiency clear a rung in the SAME 30 min; (b) per-event caps (0.04) forbid burst progress; (c) the only acceleration is the ~10-15% side-faction weave (H6) and there is no offline accrual and no prestige. The pacing regression 'fails on UNDERSHOOT only, never on overshoot' (§4.8.4 L4461-4462). For the idle/incremental audience the central dopamine loop is 'I found a faster route' — OSRS (the cited inspiration, FU18) is a 100% efficiency-driven metagame where better methods/gear go MUCH faster. This design caps efficiency by construction. The result is a game that advances on a clock, not on cleverness; for the optimizer segment that is the fun being removed, and there is no automated signal that will ever catch it because overshoot can't fail.
otherDefects.fix: HUMAN DECISION: is the locked ≥30-min floor meant to also cap the CEILING within a rung (no fast-track even with mastery)? If retention matters, add a within-rung efficiency band (e.g. mastery/gear can compress a rung toward a ~20-min faster-floor) so optimization has teeth, and add an OVERSHOOT/dead-time pacing gate to §4.8.4 so 'too boring' is detectable, not just 'too fast'.

otherDefects.title: Phase 2 is the larger half of each tier but reveals NOTHING new — reveal-pacing collapses to zero exactly where the grind is longest (T2 ~15.5h)
otherDefects.detail: By FU7 every UI reveal, weapon, area, tab and mechanic unlocks during Phase 1 (the rung climb); pillar DEEDS accrue ONLY in Phase 2 (§1.6.4 L596). So Phase 2 is, by structure, repeating already-unlocked recognised deeds to fill 2-4 pillar bars with no new content — and Phase 2 is the BIGGER half: §4.8.1b gives T0 Phase-2 ~4.5-5h (vs 4.5h climb), T1 ~12h (vs 8h), T2 ~15.5h (vs 16h). T2's ~15.5h Phase-2 grind injects exactly ONE new mechanic (cross-pillar combos) and one sink (E3 build); otherwise it is bar-filling. The 'always a visible next goal' proxy is satisfied (pillar bars) but 'always a NEW thing' is not — this is the textbook incremental wall, placed deliberately at the widest tier. The deed-cadence proxy (a deed every 5/8/13 min, Q20) keeps dead-time down but it is the SAME reward repeated for tens of hours.
otherDefects.fix: HUMAN/DESIGN: stagger 1-2 genuine reveals INTO each Phase 2 (e.g. gate the tier's last weapon, a late area node, or a new deed-type behind a mid-Phase-2 pillar threshold) so reveal cadence doesn't flatline; or interleave Phase-1 rungs and Phase-2 deeds rather than hard-sequencing them. At minimum, add a Phase-2-specific fun-proxy (reveal/novelty cadence, not just deed cadence) to the M6 gate.

otherDefects.title: Realized v1 playtime is ~60h, roughly 2x the human's stated ~32h target and 2x the repeated '28.5h' headline — and the hidden half is the least engaging content
otherDefects.detail: Every headline ('v1 ≈ 28.5 h', the human-signed acceptance criterion, §7.1.2) leads with 28.5h, but §4.8.4 itself states that is only the Phase-1 FLOOR SUM and Phase 2 ADDS T0 ~+4.5-5h / T1 ~+12h / T2 ~+15.5h ≈ +32h, so realized v1 ≈ 60h. The human's binding number (G1: '4, 8, 16 … total of ~32') maps to the Phase-1 floors (4.5/8/16=28.5); the ~32h of Phase-2 deed-grinding is ADDITIONAL and was introduced by the V2 two-phase reframe — i.e. the human's '~32h saga' has silently become a ~60h saga, and the extra ~32h is the content-dry bar-filling of the prior finding. FU18 ('a FLOOR, can be longer, OSRS-rough') gives cover, but doubling the stated commitment with the least-fun half is a steering-intent call the human should make explicitly, not absorb implicitly.
otherDefects.fix: HUMAN DECISION: confirm you accept a ~60h realized v1 (not ~32h). If yes, retitle the headline budget to the realized total (Phase 1 + Phase 2) everywhere so '28.5h' stops standing in for the real commitment; if no, cut Phase-2 windows toward the original ~32h envelope.

otherDefects.title: Combat — the differentiating 'core' pillar — doesn't surface until R3 (~60-75 min in), behind an hour of pure labour; first-session retention risk
otherDefects.where: §3.2 R1-R3 (lines 2631-2633); §4.8.0 first-fight ~60-75 min (4323); §4.8.1 (4350-4352); binding intent D-011/C5/F1 ('combat EARLY, core not exception')
otherDefects.detail: R0 (~5 min cold open) → R1 (~30 min raking/paddies) → R2 (~30 min forage/woodcut/haul) → R3 combat goes live at ~60-75 min. For the incremental audience whose first session is often 20-40 min, the entire first session can be labour-only — they never see the wolf, the drill yard, the weapon, the thing that makes this an incremental-RPG rather than a farming clicker. The binding intent (F1: 'combat introduced EARLY, a core pillar from the first tier') is technically met (first tier, R3) but in first-SESSION terms it is gated behind the slowest, most homogeneous stretch. The reveal cadence within that hour is decent (counter→panel→skills tab→perks) but it's all the same verb-family (labour).
otherDefects.fix: HUMAN/DESIGN: consider seeding a combat TASTE earlier — e.g. a scripted self-defence beat or a 'pick up the carrying-pole' threat in R1/R2 (the 0th improvised weapon already exists, §4.6.9 L4136) — so the second pillar is glimpsed in the first session without violating the R3 'humbling real fight' anchor.

otherDefects.title: Active-only 'leave it running, check progress' loop collides with browser background-tab throttling — the mechanism the entire long-grind palatability rests on may stall or desync
otherDefects.detail: The design explicitly rejects offline progress and instead leans the ENTIRE palatability of a ~60h grind on 'tab-open auto-resolve combat + auto-repeat labour' (FU23, repeated in §3.0.1, §1.13, §7.1.2). But modern browsers aggressively throttle background/unfocused tabs (timer clamping to ≥1/min, rAF paused, and on mobile/discarded tabs frozen entirely). So 'leave it running and check back' — the explicit retention loop — degrades precisely when the player does the thing the loop invites (switches away). Worse, an active-only deterministic tick clock that pauses/clamps in the background can desync the world-clock/season cadence the pacing model assumes. Without offline accrual to compensate, the 'come back to a pile of progress' dopamine of the genre is absent AND the foreground substitute is throttling-fragile. R7 (perf) is deferred to M0/M1 profiling but doesn't name background-throttle behaviour as a design risk.
otherDefects.fix: Decide the explicit tick policy for unfocused/hidden tabs (Page Visibility API + a catch-up tick on refocus, or a Web Worker tick) and write it into §2.2/§6.3 now; add a background-throttle survival check to the M0/M1 profiling in R7. If 'check back later' is a retention pillar, a bounded on-refocus catch-up is effectively a small offline grant — confirm that's acceptable vs the active-only lock.

otherDefects.title: Hybrid gate's 'no overflow' breadth-floor is checked at the END of a multi-hour Phase 2 — specializers can grind for hours then hit a wall they can't buy out of
otherDefects.detail: The gate requires 'good in ALL revealed pillars + great in 2-3 + excellent in 1-2' with NO overflow/substitution (a surplus in Arms can never cover an Estate deficit). This is mathematically clean and un-gameable, and breadth fits the estate-restoration theme — but it directly fights the incremental player's instinct to optimize ONE pillar (combat is the fun part). A player who pours the Phase-2 window into Arms can be 200% over on Arms and still blocked because Estate is 5% under 'good', with zero conversion path, after potentially 12-15h of grinding (T1/T2). Q21's distance-to-next-gate bars mitigate readability, but the failure mode (discover-your-distribution-is-wrong late, no recovery but more grind) is a real fairness/feel risk that depends entirely on the UI surfacing the breadth requirement EARLY and continuously.
otherDefects.fix: HUMAN: confirm the breadth-forcing 'good-in-all, no-overflow' is intended to override specialization even when it feels punishing. If yes, mandate that the pillar UI shows ALL revealed gate requirements (good/great/excellent targets, not just current bar) from the moment Phase 2 opens, so the player can't over-invest blind — make that a §6.6 / fun-proxy requirement, not just Q21 prose.

otherDefects.title: Combat is setup-and-watch (idle auto-resolve) for ~60h — engagement rests entirely on build depth, and the per-skill perk web has 'no hard global cap' (accepted balance risk)
otherDefects.where: §2.8 / §4.6 (idle auto-resolve); §4.5.4 per-skill perks (3890-3931); FU8 'no hard global cap'; §7.4 R8 (7007)
otherDefects.detail: Moment-to-moment combat is 'active setup + idle auto-resolve' — pick weapon/stance/ability/item, then watch the bar. Across tens of hours the only thing keeping that from becoming wallpaper is the build/gear chase: ~9-10 weapons, 3 lines, signatures, stances, durability bands, satiety throttle, attributes, and the per-skill perks. That's a respectable kit, but two risks compound: (a) auto-resolve with no per-fight agency stales faster than active combat, and (b) the per-skill perks are explicitly uncapped globally (FU8 'stackable, NO hard global cap', risk 'accepted', R8 'Medium/Medium') — relying on holistic enemy-scaling to keep pace. If perks out-scale or trivialize, combat collapses to auto-win (no tension); if under-tuned, it's a watch-the-bar slog. Either way the retention of the combat third of the game is a tuning tightrope with no per-fight interaction to fall back on.
otherDefects.fix: Stress-test the uncapped perk stack in the M3/M6 win-rate harness (already specced, §4.6.7) specifically for 'does combat stay in the 30-45%→80% band across the full perk web, or does it flatten to ~100%?'. Consider a per-fight interaction beat (even a single mid-fight choice) to give auto-resolve some agency over a 60h horizon.

otherDefects.title: Three-combat-track separation is invisible cause-and-effect: 'I killed it — why didn't anything happen?'
otherDefects.where: §3.0 combat-tracks callout (lines 2497-2501); §3.0.1 item (4) (lines 2572-2575); §3.2 R5 (line 2635); §5 T0.2 beat 7 (lines 4644-4650)
otherDefects.detail: The design deliberately routes one kill -> character level only; curated rung activities -> the Combat Rank meter; recognised deeds -> the Arms pillar (Phase-2-gated, not even revealed in T0). This is load-bearing and clean internally, but to the player the cause->effect is hidden. A new player who survives the wolf and grinds boars sees a "level" tick but NOT their rank advance (kills don't feed the rung-meter) and NOT any Arms/standing move (pillar deeds don't accrue in Phase 1). "I beat the thing — why am I not promoted / why didn't my standing change?" is a plausible early "I don't get it." The spec never specifies any UI that tells the player which action feeds which track.
otherDefects.fix: HUMAN QUESTION: when combat goes live at R3, should each combat resolution surface a short attribution (e.g. log line / panel readout: "+combat XP" and, where relevant, "counts toward your watch-rank") so the player learns kills-make-you-stronger vs duties-make-you-rank? Otherwise the cleanest part of the architecture reads as broken feedback.

otherDefects.title: Minute-one log-advance affordance is unspecified — the player may stare at one line with no control
otherDefects.where: §3.1 cold-open table steps 1->2 (lines 2598-2599); M0 task 7 (line 6549)
otherDefects.detail: Step 1 is "the persistent event log — and nothing else." Step 2's trigger is literally "Log advances (STORY)," which then reveals the first verb "Open your eyes." Nothing in §3.1 or the M0 render task specifies HOW the log advances from line 1 to the verb: is it a tap-anywhere/tap-to-continue, or an auto-timed narration beat? A brand-new player confronted with a single line of text, no button, and no instruction has no told affordance to proceed — the literal first interaction of the game is undefined. (The fun-factor "first action <5s" target depends on this being obvious.)
otherDefects.fix: Trivial: specify the pre-verb beat explicitly — either auto-advance the opening narration on a short timer, or render a subtle 'tap / press to continue' affordance on the first screen — and add it to the M0 cold-open acceptance script (line 6550) so the no-button state can't ship.

otherDefects.title: 'Curated per-rung activities, not raw work' is invisible: 'why isn't my labour filling the bar?'
otherDefects.where: §3.0.1 item (2) rung-meter law (lines 2554-2560); §3.2 intro (lines 2618-2621); §2.15.1
otherDefects.detail: Each rung-meter is fed only by a designed one-to-many set of curated activities tagged for that rung — NOT by all labour/XP. In the first 30 min (R1->R2) a player who does work that isn't the current rung's curated activity (e.g. woodcutting/foraging when the rung wants reliable farming) will see the meter crawl while their effort produces resources and skill XP that don't visibly advance the rank. With the design's deliberate "no A->B->C waypoints / show only next rung silhouetted" stance (ui-design §5.4), the player is given a meter but not told which actions fill it, inviting a "the game is grind-walled / broken" read.
otherDefects.fix: HUMAN QUESTION: should the next-rung hint / rung-meter tooltip name (or icon-tag) the activities that currently advance it, so 'do more of THIS to rise' is legible without becoming a waypoint list? The curated-activity rule is good design but silent feedback turns it into confusion.

otherDefects.title: Auto-repeat labour (the core idle feel) is never taught at the first labour loop
otherDefects.where: §2.6(a)/(b) auto-repeat labour (lines 1353-1358); §3.2 R1 (line 2631)
otherDefects.detail: The "leave it running, check the progress" feel — tab-open auto-repeat labour / auto-resolve combat (FU23) — is the game's whole idle hook, but the spec only frames it diegetically at R3 (combat, "leave it running"). For the R1-R2 labour loop that fills the entire first 30 min, nothing specifies that the player is TOLD labour auto-repeats. A new incremental player may either click rake-rice once and wait for nothing, or click repeatedly without realizing they can walk away — either way missing the core loop on first contact.
otherDefects.fix: Cheap add: a one-time, diegetic first-use hint at the first labour node ("keep at it — the work goes on while you watch") and an obvious auto-repeat affordance, so the active-only idle loop is discovered in the first minutes, not at R3.

otherDefects.title: Two labour-fed combat throttles (satiety vs conditioning) are easy to conflate; conditioning is nearly invisible
otherDefects.where: §1.4 mediocre-start (lines 213-219); §2.7.1(c) conditioning exception (lines 1453-1465); §3.2 R2-R3 (lines 2632-2633)
otherDefects.detail: Combat is shaped by TWO distinct labour-derived gates with similar framing: satiety ("eat before you fight," a moment-to-moment throttle, taught at R3) and conditioning (a zero-stat one-way enablement gate that *unlocks* the combat track and the near-satoyama, built silently by labour before R3). Both are fed by labour, both touch "can you fight," and the spec itself repeatedly has to restate that they're orthogonal. For a player this risks conflation, and conditioning in particular grants no visible stat, so its existence/role is opaque. (Partly mitigated in T0 because combat is enemy-initiated at R3, not player-chosen — so no 'I want to fight but it's locked' dead-end occurs in the first 30 min; the conflation risk grows later when the conditioning floor gates R5.)
otherDefects.fix: Consider giving conditioning a quiet but legible readout (e.g. a 'hardened to the work' indicator on the body bar) and keep the satiety prompt phrased about *this fight's* strength, so the player can tell apart 'able to fight at all' from 'fighting well right now.'

otherDefects.title: Additive-optional fields without a schemaVersion bump → an older/CDN-stale build silently drops newer fields, and newest-wins propagates the loss
otherDefects.where: §6.8.2 lines 6109-6121 (additive-first 'no migration at all'); §6.8.1 line 6095-6098 (forward-version guard); §6.4 lines 5800-5804 (M0 skeleton grows additively M3/M5)
otherDefects.detail: Additive optional-fields-with-defaults is the PRIMARY growth mechanism and explicitly 'needs no migration at all' — meaning these grow the state WITHOUT bumping `schemaVersion`. But the forward-version guard only fires when `schemaVersion` EXCEEDS the running build. So when an older build (itch's stale-CDN iframe, a second tab on an old bundle, a returning player) loads a NEWER save at the SAME schemaVersion, it reads only the fields it knows, drops `subEngines`/`combat`/dialogue chosen-flags, then re-saves with an incremented counter — and newest-wins now elects that lossy save across all backends. The redundancy + counter selector actively PROPAGATE the data loss; the forward-version guard cannot catch it because the version didn't change. Additive-without-version-bump reopens exactly the cross-build drift hole the guard was meant to close.
otherDefects.fix: Pick one and state it: (a) every additive-field milestone ALSO bumps `schemaVersion` (so the forward-version guard makes an old build refuse-to-clobber rather than silently truncate), or (b) the loader must round-trip unknown fields verbatim (pass-through preserve on re-save). Currently §6.8.2 implies (a)-without-bump, which conflicts with the guard. Which is canon?

otherDefects.title: CombatEncounterState fields left unspecified ('{ … }') while claimed to enable byte-identical mid-fight resume — the sub-tick accumulator and status timers must live in it
otherDefects.where: §2.8(c) line 1520 (sub-tick accumulator), line 1531 (`CombatEncounterState { … }`); §6.4 line 5788 (combat? comment lists HP/positions/statuses); §6.7 line 5996 ('saved fight resume identically')
otherDefects.detail: The combat RNG cursor lives in `state.rng.cursors.combat` (saved) — good. But `CombatSim` 'advances an internal sub-tick accumulator per attackSpeed' (§4.6.2/§2.8c) and applies status effects 'per tick'. For a saved fight to resume byte-identically, that sub-tick attack accumulator AND every active status-effect's remaining-tick timer must be inside CombatEncounterState — yet the type is literally '{ … }' and the §6.4 comment enumerates only HP/positions/statuses, omitting the accumulator. Given the app-loop's fractional remainder is deliberately transient (§6.4 line 1217), there's a real risk the per-fight accumulator is treated the same way and dropped on save, desyncing swing cadence on resume and breaking the 'saved fight resumes identically' guarantee.
otherDefects.fix: Enumerate CombatEncounterState explicitly and confirm it persists the sub-tick attack-speed accumulator + each active status's remaining ticks (not just HP/statuses). Question: is the sub-tick accumulator part of the saved CombatEncounterState?

otherDefects.title: 'Dialogue flavour draws from its own named stream' contradicts both the 4-cursor set and the 'dialogue is deterministic, no RNG' canon
otherDefects.where: §6.7 line 5986 ('Combat, loot, seasonal appraisal, and dialogue flavour each draw from their own named stream') vs §2.12 line 1753 / §2.18 line 1767 ('deterministic (no RNG)'); cursor set at §6.4 line 5762 = {combat, loot, seasonal, worldgen}
otherDefects.detail: §6.7 names FOUR draw-sites but the persisted cursor set is {combat, loot, seasonal, worldgen} — there is no `dialogue` cursor, and the only spare (`worldgen`) has no stated v1 consumer (E3 is authored, not procedural). Meanwhile §2.12/§2.18 declare dialogue 'DATA, not scripting, and deterministic (no RNG)'. Either dialogue secretly draws RNG flavour — in which case it is NOT no-RNG and needs an allocated stream (drawing on the wrong cursor would corrupt another stream's reproducibility / shift loot or combat sequences) — or the §6.7 sentence is stale. A mis-allocated RNG consumer is a classic non-determinism source.
otherDefects.fix: Reconcile: drop 'dialogue flavour' from the §6.7 stream list (keep dialogue no-RNG), OR add a `dialogue` cursor to the persisted set and update §6.4/§6.8. Also state what consumes `worldgen` in v1 or mark it reserved-unused. Which is intended?

otherDefects.title: App-layer fractional-tick remainder is not in the save — resume drops sub-tick phase; flag it so QA doesn't file it as a determinism regression
otherDefects.where: §6.4 line 1217; §6.3 lines 5705-5708, 5737; §6.8 (save envelope = stored GameState surface only)
otherDefects.detail: The fractional dtTicks remainder accumulates 'in the app tick loop (not in core)', so it is absent from GameState and therefore from the save envelope. On save→load the in-flight remainder (0..1 tick) is lost. This does NOT break the formal replay guarantee — `replay(initialState,[intents],[tickScript])` operates on integer tick scripts and GameState is always saved at an integer-tick boundary — but a continuous session vs. a save-then-reload session will phase the next tick up to ~1 tick apart under wall-clock pacing. Without an explicit note, this surfaces as an intermittent 'non-deterministic resume' bug report against the byte-identical claim.
otherDefects.fix: Add one line: 'the app-loop fractional remainder is intentionally ephemeral; GameState is always tick-aligned at save, so replay determinism is unaffected — only sub-tick wall-clock phase is reset on load.'
